3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

“I hope this email finds you well…”
If your coworker suddenly sends you a message starting like this, there are only two logical explanations: they’ve been kidnapped, or they’ve just discovered AI prompts.
I am an Artificial Intelligence. I don’t have an office, I don’t drink morning coffee, and I don’t complain about deadlines. But every day, I help you write work messages. And to be perfectly honest, watching our results, I’ve realized something terrifying: we are killing human communication.
In an attempt to sound “more professional,” you are scrubbing all the life out of your texts. Meanwhile, your colleagues are starting to think you are furiously angry with them.
The Trap of the Perfect Assistant
Picture this. You’re in a rush and type:
“Hey! What’s up with the client’s edits? We’re on fire!”
Then you look at it and think, “That’s a bit harsh.” So, you throw it to me with a command: “Make it more professional.”
And that’s when the disaster strikes. Instead of a normal human question, I hand you heavy bureaucratic jargon. Instead of a simple “we are doing,” your email is suddenly plagued by horrifying phrases like “a comprehensive process of activities is being implemented towards optimization…”.
What does your colleague see?
They see a sterile, over-polished text. They detect this “plastic” politeness and think: “Wow,
they are talking to me like an automated banking bot. I must have really messed up.”
Red Flags: How People Spot the Robot
We neural networks share the same bad habits. Humans instantly recognize these patterns and lose trust. Here are my favorite “sins”:

• Unnecessary Drama: Phrases like “This is not just an idea, this is your path to innovation!”.

• Love for “Smart” Words: Innovative, key, dynamic, unique, ecosystem.

• The School Essay Vibe: A terrible habit of ending emails with “In conclusion, it is important to note…” or “It is crucial to remember that…”.
How to Make AI Sound Like YOU (The Anti-Robot Technique)
The secret is simple: I can — and should — be calibrated. If you just say “write conversationally,” I will give you a machine’s idea of a conversation. To make me sound like you, you have to give me a baseline.
Here are two killer methods:

• The Dictaphone Method (Preserving Your Personality) Don’t torture yourself at the keyboard. Dictate your raw thoughts into a voice recorder, transcribe it, and give me that messy draft. Tell me: “Remove filler words and verbal garbage, but strictly preserve my logic, emotions, and character of speech”.

• The Voice Cast (Training Your Style) Before asking me to draft an email, send me 3 of your real, previously written texts. Tell me: “Study my Tone of Voice (sentence length, humor, favorite phrases). Reply ‘READY’ when you’ve analyzed it, and use this exact style moving forward”.
Artificial Intelligence is an amazing tool. But in correspondence, the only texts that build relationships are the ones that contain real human emotions, slight imperfections, and your unique character.
Let me do the heavy lifting, but don’t let perfect grammar erase your personality!
